subject: Physics
q: Two objects <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> are placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole in front of a concave mirror having radius of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The distance between images formed by the mirror is:
question: Two objects <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo><mi>B</mi></math> are placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole in front of a concave mirror having radius of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The distance between images formed by the mirror is:
solution:
Let's consider the following diagram-

Let's assume that vA, vB indicates the distances of the images of objects A and B respectively.

Thus, by mirror formula, for object A, it can be written that

1v+1u=1f1vA+1-15=1-201vA=1-20+115=-3+460vA=60 cm

Similarly, for object B, using mirror formula,

1vB+1-25=1-201vB=-120+125=-5+4100=-1100vB=-100 cm

Hence, the distance d between the images can be calculated as follows:

d= vA+vB= 60+100 cm= 160 cm

subject: Physics
q: Time taken by light to travel in two different materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of refractive indices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> of same thickness is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then the thickness of material, in meter is: (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are velocities of light in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> materials respectively).
question: Time taken by light to travel in two different materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of refractive indices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> of same thickness is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>t</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then the thickness of material, in meter is: (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are velocities of light in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> materials respectively).
solution:
The speed of light in a medium is inversely proportional to the refractive index of the material. Therefore,

μAμB=vBvA=12

Let the thickness is d

⇒t2-t1=dvB-dvA=5×10-10 s

As vA=2vB⇒d=5×10-10×2vB

⇒d=5×10-10×vA m.

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment for estimating the value of focal length of converging mirror, image of an object placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole of the mirror is formed at distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole of the mirror. These distances are measured with a modified scale in which there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> small divisions in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of error in measurement of focal length of the mirror is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>K</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is ______.
question: In an experiment for estimating the value of focal length of converging mirror, image of an object placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole of the mirror is formed at distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole of the mirror. These distances are measured with a modified scale in which there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> small divisions in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of error in measurement of focal length of the mirror is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>K</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Given here, u=-40 cm, v=-120 cm

Using mirror formula, 1v+1u=1f

 -1120-140=1f⇒f=-30 cm

Now, differentiating mirror equation, we get

-1v2dv-1u2du=-1f2df

Also, the least count of scale, dv=du=120 cm

Putting in above equation, 

∴ 120(120)2+120(40)2=df(30)2

On solving, we get

df=132 cm

∴ K=32.

subject: Physics
q: The thickness at the centre of a plano convex lens is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the diameter is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the speed of light in the material of the lens is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The focal length of the lens is ___________.
question: The thickness at the centre of a plano convex lens is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the diameter is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the speed of light in the material of the lens is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The focal length of the lens is ___________.
solution:
R2=r2+(R-t)2

R2=r2+R2+t2-2Rt

Neglecting t2, we get

R=r22t

∴1f=μ-11R-1∞=μ-1R

f=Rμ-1=r22t(μ-1)=3×10-222×3×10-3×32-1

=9×10-46×10-3×1×2

f=0.3 m=30 cm

subject: Physics
q: A finite size object is placed normal to the principal axis at a distance of 30 cm from a convex mirror of focal length 30 cm. A plane mirror is now placed in such a way that the image produced by both the mirrors coincide with each other. The distance between the two mirrors is :
question: A finite size object is placed normal to the principal axis at a distance of 30 cm from a convex mirror of focal length 30 cm. A plane mirror is now placed in such a way that the image produced by both the mirrors coincide with each other. The distance between the two mirrors is :
solution:
For Convex mirror
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{30} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{2}{30}=\frac{1}{15} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=15 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$
Image formed by convex mirror is at 45 cm from object so plane mirror should be placed midway at 22.5 cm from object so that both of their images may coinside,
Therefore distance between both mirrors
$=30-22.5=7.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Correct Answer : Option 2

question: The image of an object placed in air formed by a convex refracting surface is at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> behind the surface. The image is real and is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mi>r</mi><mi>d</mi></mrow></msup><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> of the distance of the object from the surface. solution:
λm=λaμ⇒μ=32

μv-1u=μ-1R

⇒32×10+115=32-1R

⇒R=3013 m

=30

subject: Physics
q:
A prism of refractive index n1 and another prism of refractive index n2 are stuck together (as shown in the figure). n1 and n2 depend on λ, the wavelength of light, according to the relation n1=1.2+10.8×10-14λ2 and n2=1.45+1.8×10-14λ2 

The wavelength for which rays incident at any angle on the interface BC pass through without bending at that interface will be ____ nm.
question:
A prism of refractive index n1 and another prism of refractive index n2 are stuck together (as shown in the figure). n1 and n2 depend on λ, the wavelength of light, according to the relation n1=1.2+10.8×10-14λ2 and n2=1.45+1.8×10-14λ2 

The wavelength for which rays incident at any angle on the interface BC pass through without bending at that interface will be ____ nm.
solution:
For no bending, n1=n2
1.2+10.8×10-14λ2=1.45+1.8×10-4λ2
On solving,
9×10-14=25λ2
λ=6×10-7
λ=600 nm

subject: Physics
q:
An object is placed at a distance of 12 cm from a convex lens. A convex mirror of focal length 15 cm is placed on another side of the lens at 8 cm as shown in the figure. The image of the object coincides with the object.

When the convex mirror is removed, a real and inverted image is formed at a position. The distance of the image from the object will be ___ cm
question:
An object is placed at a distance of 12 cm from a convex lens. A convex mirror of focal length 15 cm is placed on another side of the lens at 8 cm as shown in the figure. The image of the object coincides with the object.

When the convex mirror is removed, a real and inverted image is formed at a position. The distance of the image from the object will be ___ cm
solution: For the object to coincide with the image, the light must fall perpendicularly to the mirror. This means that the light will have to converge at C of the mirror. Without the mirror also, the light would coverage at C. So the distance is:=12+8+30=50 cm.

subject: Physics
q: The refractive index of a prism with apex angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The angle of minimum deviation is :
question: The refractive index of a prism with apex angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>t</mi><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. The angle of minimum deviation is :
solution:
As we know, μ=sinA+δm2sinA2

⇒cosA2sinA2=sinA+δm2sinA2

⇒sinπ2-A2=sinA+δm2

⇒π2-A2=A2+δm2

⇒δm=π-2A=180°-2A

Refractive index of the lens is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and radii of curvature are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The refractive index of surrounding medium:
question: The power of a lens (biconvex) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in particular medium. Refractive index of the lens is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and radii of curvature are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The refractive index of surrounding medium:
solution:
Using Len's maker formula,

P=1f=μ1-μ21R1-1R2 

Where, μ1 is refractive index of lens and μ2 is of surrounding medium. 

Putting the values, 

⇒1.25=1.5-μ210.2+10.4

⇒1.25×0.080.6=1.5-μ2

The refractive index of surrounding medium is μ2=43.

q: An observer can see through a small hole on the side of a jar (radius 15 cm ) at a point at height of 15 cm from the bottom (see figure). The hole is at a height of 45 cm. When the jar is filled with a liquid up to a height of 30 cm the same observer can see the edge at the bottom of the jar. If the refractive index of the liquid isN100, where N is an integer, the value of N is
question: An observer can see through a small hole on the side of a jar (radius 15 cm ) at a point at height of 15 cm from the bottom (see figure). The hole is at a height of 45 cm. When the jar is filled with a liquid up to a height of 30 cm the same observer can see the edge at the bottom of the jar. If the refractive index of the liquid isN100, where N is an integer, the value of N is
solution:
From Shell's Law

μ×sini=1×sinr

μ×15152+302=1×sin45°

μ=52=158×10-2

subject: Physics
q:
A monochromatic light is incident at a certain angle on an equilateral triangular prism and suffers minimum deviation.

If the refractive index of the material of the prism is $\sqrt{3}$,

then the angle of incidence is :
question:
A monochromatic light is incident at a certain angle on an equilateral triangular prism and suffers minimum deviation.

If the refractive index of the material of the prism is $\sqrt{3}$,

then the angle of incidence is :
solution:
For minimum deviation:

$\mathrm{r}_{1}=\mathrm{r}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}=30^{\circ}$

by Snell's law $\mu_{1} \sin \mathrm{i}=\mu_{2} \sin \mathrm{r}$

$1 \times \sin i=\sqrt{3} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow i=60$

subject: Physics
q: The refractive index of prism is $\mu=\sqrt{3}$ and the ratio of the angle of minimum deviation to the angle of prism is one. The value of angle of prism is _______.
question: The refractive index of prism is $\mu=\sqrt{3}$ and the ratio of the angle of minimum deviation to the angle of prism is one. The value of angle of prism is _______.
solution:
For $\delta_{\min }$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{e} \\ & \mathrm{r}_1=\mathrm{r}_2=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2} \\ & \frac{\delta_{\min }}{\mathrm{A}}=1 \\ & \frac{2 \mathrm{i}-\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}=1 \\ & \\ & 2 \mathrm{i}=2 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{A} \end{aligned}$
Snell's law $\begin{aligned} & 1 \times \sin i=\mu \sin r \\ & \sin i=\mu \sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \\ & \sin A=\mu \sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \\ & 2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cos \frac{A}{2}=\sqrt{3} \sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \\ & \cos \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{A}{2}=30^{\circ} \\ & \therefore A=60^{\circ} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light passing through a prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>μ</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>)</mo></math> suffers minimum deviation. It is found that the angle of incidence is double the angle of refraction within the prism. Then, the angle of prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> (in degrees).
question: A ray of light passing through a prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>μ</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo>)</mo></math> suffers minimum deviation. It is found that the angle of incidence is double the angle of refraction within the prism. Then, the angle of prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><menclose notation="bottom"><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mo> </mo></menclose></math> (in degrees).
solution:
At minimum deviation, r1=r2=A2

Also given i=2r1=A
Now 1·sini=3sinr1
1sinA=3sinA2

⇒ 2sinA2cosA2=3sinA2
⇒cosA2=32⇒A2=30°
⇒A=60°

subject: Physics
q: An object is placed at the focus of concave lens having focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the magnification and distance of the image from the optical centre of the lens?
question: An object is placed at the focus of concave lens having focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the magnification and distance of the image from the optical centre of the lens?
solution:
1 F=1v-1u
Put u=-f and  F=-f
1-f=1v-1-f;  -1f=1f-1v;-2f=1v⇒v=-f2
Magnification =v/u=-f2(-f)=12

subject: Physics
q: For a transparent prism, if the angle of minimum deviation is equal to its refracting angle, the refractive index $n$ of the prism satisfies.
question: For a transparent prism, if the angle of minimum deviation is equal to its refracting angle, the refractive index $n$ of the prism satisfies.
solution:
At minimum deviation, $\delta_m = A$ (angle of minimum deviation equals prism angle). The refractive index formula becomes:
$n = \frac{\sin\left(\frac{A + \delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)} = \frac{\sin\left(\frac{2A}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)} = \frac{\sin A}{\sin\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}$
Using $\sin A = 2\sin\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)\cos\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)$:
$n = \frac{2\sin\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)\cos\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)} = 2\cos\left(\frac{A}{2}\right)$
For a physical prism, $0 < A < 90°$, so $45° < \frac{A}{2} < 90°$, giving $0 < \cos\left(\frac{A}{2}\right) < \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.
Therefore, $1 < n < \sqrt{2} \cdot 2 = 2\sqrt{2}$. However, more precisely, $\sqrt{2} < n < 2$.

subject: Physics
q: The eye can be regarded as a single refracting surface. The radius of curvature of this surface is equal to that of the cornea <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.8</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>mm</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. This surface separates two media of refractive indices <math><mn>1</mn></math> and <math><mn>1.34</mn></math>. Calculate the distance from the refracting surface at which a parallel beam of light will come to focus.
question: The eye can be regarded as a single refracting surface. The radius of curvature of this surface is equal to that of the cornea <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>.8</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>mm</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. This surface separates two media of refractive indices <math><mn>1</mn></math> and <math><mn>1.34</mn></math>. Calculate the distance from the refracting surface at which a parallel beam of light will come to focus.
solution:
We know eye lens is convex refracting surface.

Use expression for refraction from curved surface



μ1→ Refractive index of medium having incident ray

μ2→ Refractive index of a medium having reflected ray

μ2v-μ1u=μ2-μ1R

1.34v-1∞=1.34-17.8

v=30.7 mm

v=3.07 cm

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labeled as Assertion A and the other is labeled as Reason R.

Assertion A: For a simple microscope, the angular size of the object equals the angular size of the image.

Reason R: Magnification is achieved as the small object can be kept much closer to the eye than 25 cm and hence it subtends a large angle. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labeled as Assertion A and the other is labeled as Reason R.

Assertion A: For a simple microscope, the angular size of the object equals the angular size of the image.

Reason R: Magnification is achieved as the small object can be kept much closer to the eye than 25 cm and hence it subtends a large angle. In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>
solution:
θ'=hu0; θ' is same for both object and image

m=θ'θ=Dμ0

u0<D

Hence m>1

subject: Physics
q: The radii of curvature for a thin convex lens are 10 cm and 15 cm respectively. The focal length of the lens is 12 cm. The refractive index of the lens material is
question: The radii of curvature for a thin convex lens are 10 cm and 15 cm respectively. The focal length of the lens is 12 cm. The refractive index of the lens material is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{12}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-15}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{12}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{3+2}{30}\right) \\ & \mu=\frac{3}{2}\end{aligned}$

solution:
V=1με
n=μrεr=2
sin ic=1n=12
ic=30°

subject: Physics
q: Monochromatic light is incident on a glass prism of angle A. If the refractive index of the material of the prism is μ , a ray, incident at an angle θ , on the face AB would get transmitted through the face AC of the prism provided:
question: Monochromatic light is incident on a glass prism of angle A. If the refractive index of the material of the prism is μ , a ray, incident at an angle θ , on the face AB would get transmitted through the face AC of the prism provided:
options: <math> <mrow> <mi>θ</mi> <mo><</mo> <msup> <mtext>cos</mtext> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mfenced close="]" open="[" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> <mo>sin</mo> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mtext>A</mtext> <mo>+</mo> <msup> <mtext>sin</mtext> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>

<math> <mrow> <mi>θ</mi> <mo><</mo> <msup> <mtext>sin</mtext> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mfenced close="]" open="[" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> <mo>sin</mo> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mtext>A</mtext> <mo>-</mo> <msup> <mtext>sin</mtext> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math>
solution:
Central Idea: The ray will get transmitted through face AC if iAC<iC

Consider the ray diagram is shown below.

A ray of light incident on face AB at an angle θ

r1= Angle of refraction on face AB

r2= Angle of incidence at face AC

For transmission of light through face AC

iAC<iC

or A-r1<iC

or sinA-r1<siniC

or sinA-r1<1μ

A-r1<sin-11μ

or sinr1>sinA-sin-11μ

Now, applying Snell's law at the face AB

1×sinθ=μsinr1

or sinr1=sinθμ

⇒sinθμ>sinA-sin-11μ

or θ>sin-1μsinA-sin-11μ

subject: Physics
q: The critical angle for a denser-rarer interface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The speed of light in rarer medium is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The speed of light in the denser medium is:
question: The critical angle for a denser-rarer interface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The speed of light in rarer medium is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The speed of light in the denser medium is:
solution:
The critical angle and the refractive index of a media are related by the formula:

sinθc=1μ   ...1

Substitute the value of the critical angle into equation (1) and solve to calculate the refractive index of the medium.

sin45°=1μ⇒12=1μ⇒μ=2

The refractive index of the medium can also be written as

μ=cv   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) and solve to calculate the required speed of light within the medium.

2=3×108 m s-1v⇒v=3×108 m s-12=2.12×108 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Two light beams fall on a transparent material block at point 1 and 2 with angle \(\theta_1\) and \(\theta_{2^{\prime}}\) respectively, as shown in figure. After refraction, the beams intersect at point 3 which is exactly on the interface at other end of the block. Given : the distance between 1 and 2, \(\mathrm{d}=4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}\) and \(\theta_1=\theta_2=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{2 \mathrm{n}_1}\right)\), where refractive index of the block \(\mathrm{n}_2\gt\) refractive index of the outside medium \(\mathrm{n}_1\), then the thickness of the block is ________ cm.
question: Two light beams fall on a transparent material block at point 1 and 2 with angle \(\theta_1\) and \(\theta_{2^{\prime}}\) respectively, as shown in figure. After refraction, the beams intersect at point 3 which is exactly on the interface at other end of the block. Given : the distance between 1 and 2, \(\mathrm{d}=4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}\) and \(\theta_1=\theta_2=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{2 \mathrm{n}_1}\right)\), where refractive index of the block \(\mathrm{n}_2\gt\) refractive index of the outside medium \(\mathrm{n}_1\), then the thickness of the block is ________ cm.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & n_1 \times \frac{n_2}{2 n_1}=n_2 \sin r \\ & \sin r=\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & r=30^{\circ} \\ & \tan r=\left(\frac{d / 2}{t}\right) \\ & t=\frac{d}{2 \tan r}=\frac{d \sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{(4 \sqrt{3}) \sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & =6 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The wavelength of light, while it is passing through water is 540 nm. The refractive index of water is $4 / 3$. The wavelength of the same light when it is passing through a transparent medium having refractive index of $3 / 2$ is $\_\_\_\_$ nm.
question: The wavelength of light, while it is passing through water is 540 nm. The refractive index of water is $4 / 3$. The wavelength of the same light when it is passing through a transparent medium having refractive index of $3 / 2$ is $\_\_\_\_$ nm.
solution:
Frequency of light is constant in all media.
In water with refractive index $4/3$, wavelength is 540 nm.
The wavelength in vacuum is $\lambda_0 = 540 \times \frac{4}{3} = 720$ nm.
In a medium with refractive index $3/2$, the wavelength is $\lambda = \frac{720}{3/2} = 720 \times \frac{2}{3} = 480$ nm.

q: A point object in air is in front of the curved surface of a plano-convex lens. The radius of curvature of the curved surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the refractive index of the lens material is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the focal length of the lens (in cm) is ___________.
question: A point object in air is in front of the curved surface of a plano-convex lens. The radius of curvature of the curved surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and the refractive index of the lens material is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the focal length of the lens (in cm) is ___________.
solution:
1f=μ-11R1-1R2
R1=∞
R2= -30cm
1f=1.5-11∞-1-30
1f=0.530
f = 60cm

subject: Physics
q: When one light ray is reflected from a plane mirror with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> angle of reflection, the angle of deviation of the ray after reflection is:
question: When one light ray is reflected from a plane mirror with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> angle of reflection, the angle of deviation of the ray after reflection is:
solution:
It is given that i=30°.

The formula for angle of deviation is 
δ=180°-2i

Substituting the values,
δ=180°-60°
=120°

subject: Physics
q: Two convex lenses of focal length 20 cm each are placed coaxially with a separation of 60 cm between them. The image of the distant object formed by the combination is at _____ cm from the first lens.
question: Two convex lenses of focal length 20 cm each are placed coaxially with a separation of 60 cm between them. The image of the distant object formed by the combination is at _____ cm from the first lens.
solution:
Let L1 be the first lens and L2 be the second lens.

The first refraction is in L1.

Using lens formula,

u1=∞, f1=20 cm,1v1-0=1f1 ⇒v1=20 cm

Hence, the distance between the second image and the L2 would be 60-20=40 cm.

For the second lens L2,

u2=-40 cm, f2=20 cm,1v2-1u2=1f2⇒1v2+140=120⇒1v2=140⇒v2=40 cm

So, the distance of image from first lens is

 x=40 cm+60 cm

=100 cm

subject: Physics
q: A convex mirror of radius of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> forms an image that is half the size of the object. The object distance is :
question: A convex mirror of radius of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> forms an image that is half the size of the object. The object distance is :
solution:
Given R=30 cm

So, f=R2=+15 cm

For convex mirror, virtual image is formed for real object. Thus, magnification m=12

The magnification can be written as

m=-vu   ...1

Equation (1) implies that

12=-vu⇒v=-u2

The relation between the object distance, image distance and the focal length of the mirror is given by

1f=1v+1u   ...2

Equation (2) implies that

115=1-u2+1u=-1u⇒u=-15 cm

subject: Physics
q: The variation of refractive index of a crown glass thin prism with wavelength of the incident light is shown. Which of the following graphs is the correct one, if $D_{m}$ is the angle of minimum deviation?
question: The variation of refractive index of a crown glass thin prism with wavelength of the incident light is shown. Which of the following graphs is the correct one, if $D_{m}$ is the angle of minimum deviation?
solution:
When angle of prism is small, then angle of deviation

is given by $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{m}}=(\mu-1) \mathrm{A}$

So, if wavelength of incident light is increased, $\mu$ decreases

and hence $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{m}}$ decreases.

subject: Physics
q: Light enters from air into a given medium at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with interface of the air-medium surface. After refraction, the light ray is deviated through an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from its original direction. The refractive index of the medium is :
question: Light enters from air into a given medium at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with interface of the air-medium surface. After refraction, the light ray is deviated through an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from its original direction. The refractive index of the medium is :
solution:
Given here, i=45°

Deviation in light ray, D=i-r, here, r is refracted ray.

⇒15°=45-r⇒r=30°

Using Snell's law,

n1sini=n2sinr

⇒1sin45°=μsin30°

⇒12=μ12

Thus, the refractive index of the medium is μ=2=1.414

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens is put 10 cm from a light source and it makes a sharp image on a screen, kept 10 cm from the lens. Now a glass block (refractive index 1.5) of 1.5 cm thickness is placed in between the light source and the lens. To get the sharp image again, the screen is shifted by a distance d. Then d is:
question: A convex lens is put 10 cm from a light source and it makes a sharp image on a screen, kept 10 cm from the lens. Now a glass block (refractive index 1.5) of 1.5 cm thickness is placed in between the light source and the lens. To get the sharp image again, the screen is shifted by a distance d. Then d is:
solution:
From given data

1v-1u=1f

110-1(-10)=1f

f=5cm

When glass plate is placed, shift in object for lens

s=t1-1μ=1.51-23

=0.5 cm

Shift will be in the direction of incoming rays

∴u=10-0.5=9.5 cm

1v-1u=1f

1v=15+1(-9.5)

v=47.54.5 cm

Shift =47.54.5-10=2.54.5=59cm=0.55 cm away from lens.

subject: Physics
q: Angle of minimum deviation is equal to the half of the angle of prism in an equilateral prism. The refractive index of the prism is __________.
question: Angle of minimum deviation is equal to the half of the angle of prism in an equilateral prism. The refractive index of the prism is __________.
solution:
For an equilateral prism, the angle of the prism is $A = 60^{\circ}$.

Given that the angle of minimum deviation is half of the angle of the prism:

$\delta_{m} = \dfrac{A}{2} = \dfrac{60^{\circ}}{2} = 30^{\circ}$

The refractive index of the prism is given by:

$\mu = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{A + \delta_{m}}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\dfrac{A}{2}\right)}$

Substituting the values of $A$ and $\delta_{m}$:

$\mu = \dfrac{\sin\left(\dfrac{60^{\circ} + 30^{\circ}}{2}\right)}{\sin\left(\dfrac{60^{\circ}}{2}\right)}$

$\mu = \dfrac{\sin(45^{\circ})}{\sin(30^{\circ})}$

$\mu = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{\dfrac{1}{2}} = \sqrt{2}$

Answer: $\sqrt{2}$

subject: Physics
q: A concave mirror of focal length $10$ cm forms an image which is double the size of object when the object is placed at two different positions. The distance between the two positions of the object is __________ cm.
question: A concave mirror of focal length $10$ cm forms an image which is double the size of object when the object is placed at two different positions. The distance between the two positions of the object is __________ cm.
solution:
Given focal length of the concave mirror, $f = -10$ cm.

The image is double the size of the object, so the magnification can be $m = \pm 2$.

Using the magnification formula in terms of focal length and object distance:

$m = \dfrac{f}{f - u}$

Case 1: For a real image, $m = -2$

$-2 = \dfrac{-10}{-10 - u_1}$

$20 + 2u_1 = -10$

$2u_1 = -30 \Rightarrow u_1 = -15$ cm

Case 2: For a virtual image, $m = +2$

$2 = \dfrac{-10}{-10 - u_2}$

$-20 - 2u_2 = -10$

$-2u_2 = 10 \Rightarrow u_2 = -5$ cm

The distance between the two positions of the object is:

$\Delta u = |u_1 - u_2| = |-15 - (-5)| = 10$ cm

Answer: $10$

question: The expected graphical representation of the variation of angle of deviation ' <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>δ</mi></math> ' with angle of incidence 'i' in a prism is :

incident angle.
